next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 17

By seven the house smelled expensive and overworked. Lilies heated too close to the radiators had gone thick and sweet in the front hall, and under them lived the smells nobody put on invitations: dishwasher steam from the basement, frying oil from the caterers' burners in the mews, bleach, wet wool, the sour metal breath of lids lifted and dropped over chafing dishes. Beni moved between the front step and the garage with a radio clipped under his lapel and rain drying in a cold stripe down his back. Every room upstairs looked effortless because three rooms below it were not.

The gala had eaten the whole townhouse. The library tables were gone under white cloth and auction cards. The morning room had become a champagne station. The drawing room held a small forest of hired trees, pale roses wired into them so tightly they looked pinned there by force. On the easel by the stairs a cream card explained, in a font that managed to seem both charitable and expensive, what the evening would do for women and children in emergency accommodation. One table funded six weeks. One premier bid funded a whole room. Beni had seen Eszter's church-basement bed the night before, the blanket folded with church neatness and the metal chair that left dents in the wall when you scraped it back. He read the number once and did not read it again.

The staff moved with the flat, shut face of people who could not afford a mistake. A coat-check woman from the agency kept blotting at her nose with the inside of her wrist because the lilies had started her hay fever, then smiling at fur-...
open_hooks_to_carry: Build suspense through preparation, timing, and dread rather than through action mechanics.
Use service corridors, delivery doors, and staff choreography to literalize the novel's class structure.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...r had wrapped her feet in it. Small domestic wreckage already, proof of a real body passing through. Not a donor photo, not a scandal, not a lesson. Just a wet, angry girl who needed somewhere to sleep.

"I'll keep my phone on," he said.

"You already said that," Eszter replied.

"Right."

He put his hand on the door. Bea looked up once more. "Beni."

He waited.

"I know this doesn't fix anything."

He thought of the garage, of the kitchen after Bea had spoken, of Eszter's face when she understood what kind of money he had pressed at her problems, of all the clean lies he had told because speaking late always felt easier than speaking in time. "No," he said. "It doesn't."

Bea nodded, apparently satisfied by that much honesty. Eszter was already turning down the bed and pulling the church blanket free with sharp economical snaps. Her pink elastic flashed once on her wrist in the yellow light.

Beni stepped out into the corridor. The door stayed open just long enough for Eszter to say, without looking at him, "Bring back Laci's umbrella tomorrow if you want one decent thing not to rot on top of the others."

"I will."

Then she shut the door.

He stood under the buzzing basement light with the damp umbrella in his hand and the smell of lentils and paprika leaking faintly through the wood. Inside, he heard Eszter say, "Move over, your wet hair will soak the pillow," and Bea answer something too soft to catch. It was not forgiveness. It was a lock, a bowl in the sink, a bed being made wider by necessity. He went up the stairs before he could turn any of it into more than that.
state_deltas_required: Get through gala preparations and decide whether Nina's offer of Paris is rescue or erasure.
Beni privately rejects Nina's offer after recognizing it as a more elegant version of the side-door life he has been living.
